subtitle: Otau, Kara uy
description: Traditional portable lattice-frame dwelling, perfectly adapted to a semi-nomadic way of life. The Karakalpak yurt differs from its Kazakh and Turkmen neighbours by a more conical dome and abundant carpet decoration. Built from a wooden frame (kerege, uyk, shangarak) covered with felt and reed mats (shiy).
places: Ustyurt plateau, Kyzylkum desert, near wells and natural reservoirs (kak). A common summer dwelling in fishermen's and farmers' homesteads.

subtitle: Reed-frame dwelling
description: Stationary frame dwelling with a gabled roof. Walls were built from a wooden frame filled with reed bundles (shom) and plastered with clay. Typical of regions where the soil was unsuitable for pakhsa (rammed earth).
places: Moynak district, Aral Sea coast, Amu Darya delta channels.

subtitle: Pakhsa jay, Atpa tam
description: Stationary mud-walled dwelling with a flat roof. Walls were built using the pakhsa method (layer-by-layer rammed clay). Living and utility rooms shared one roof, often with a canopy (shertek) and a space for setting up a yurt (uy jay).
places: Agricultural districts. Southern Kungrad, northern Khojeyli districts.

subtitle: Fortified house
description: Large estate of an extended undivided family, where dwelling and utility buildings were unified by a tall mud-brick wall (up to 7-8 metres) with towers (gungre). An open courtyard and a place for a yurt were always inside.
places: Along major canals: Lauzan, Suenli, Shortanbay, Kegeyli, Kuanysh-Jarma.

subtitle: Urban frame dwelling
description: An advanced type of frame dwelling, where the wooden frame was filled with reeds, raw bricks or clay lumps and then carefully plastered. Considered warmer and drier than a regular tam.
places: Mainly cities: Chimbay, Khojeyli, Kungrad, Turtkul. Also in rural areas with damp saline soil.

subtitle: Fishermen's stilt house
description: Stilt structures made of reed bundles, built on water. Used by fishermen on the Aral Sea coast.

subtitle: Tole - dugout
description: Dugouts and semi-dugouts used by the poor or as temporary shelter. Found in areas with low groundwater tables.

subtitle: Reed yurt
description: A yurt-like structure built entirely from reeds. Used by fishermen in coastal areas.

subtitle: Wedding cart
description: A wedding dwelling in the form of a covered cart on an arba, used to transport the bride. A mobile ritual structure tied to the wedding ceremony.
